message.content:
Research **Twenty CRM** (twenty.com, github.com/twentyhq/twenty, docs.twenty.com) with CURRENT (2024-2025) sources. Fetch official docs and GitHub. Cite URLs. Be honest; say "I could not find / unclear" rather than guessing.

CONTEXT: A company currently runs a FORKED Twenty (v2.8.3) in production. Their fork added: (a) ~8 custom objects (department, emailMessage, factory, ignoreRule, licensorApprovalThread, meetingNote, meetingNoteAttendee, aiModelConfig) as CODE-DEFINED standard objects; (b) many custom fields on company/person/opportunity (incl. ~23 "program" fields like PO#, factory, season, stage, buyer); (c) server-side business logic: a cron polling Microsoft Outlook via Graph API, a multi-step email-routing classifier, listeners on record create/update, a ClickUp stage-sync cron, a Fireflies webhook, AI summary generation; (d) a few FRONTEND tweaks: a company-scoped "department" relation picker (greyed out until a company is chosen, filtered to that company's departments), scoped dropdowns, a right-click context menu, a build-hash badge, and English-only i18n.

They HATE forking (the v1.20→v2.8 upgrade caused 1,437 conflicts). GOAL: move back to STOCK/upstream Twenty with ZERO fork, reproducing functionality via (1) NO-CODE runtime custom objects/fields and (2) EXTERNAL services on the API + webhooks. Tell me precisely what is and isn't possible.

Answer these:

1. **No-code custom objects & fields.** Can you create custom objects and custom fields entirely via Twenty's Settings UI or its **Metadata API** (no code)? What FIELD TYPES are supported no-code (text, number, date/datetime, boolean, single-select, multi-select, currency, relation 1:N / N:N, links, rating, etc.)? Any limits vs code-defined "standard objects"? Can no-code custom objects have relations to standard objects (company/person/opportunity)? Cite docs.

2. **API + webhooks + automation.** Confirm REST + GraphQL + a **Metadata API** (to script object/field creation and migration). Confirm **webhooks** on record create/update/delete. Is there a built-in **Workflow** engine, and can workflow steps run **custom code / serverless functions / HTTP calls** WITHOUT forking? Any rate limits / batch limits on the API (e.g., 60 records/call)?

3. **Reproducing the server-side logic externally.** Could an EXTERNAL service (its own server) do: poll Outlook (Microsoft Graph), create emailMessage records via Twenty's API, run routing classification, and write companyId/departmentId/programId back via API; sync ClickUp; receive Fireflies webhooks; generate AI summaries — all WITHOUT forking, using only the public API + webhooks? Any blockers (e.g., can external code set custom-object fields, trigger on record events via webhooks)?

4. **Frontend extensibility — THE KEY QUESTION.** Does Twenty have ANY supported way to customize/extend the FRONTEND (React app) WITHOUT forking? Specifically: custom field input/display components, custom views/pages, custom relation-picker behavior (like a company-scoped filtered dropdown), plugins, an app/extension framework, or a component SDK? Check the repo for a "twenty-sdk"/"client-sdk"/plugin system and check the public roadmap for any planned frontend extension/app-framework. If the ONLY way to change frontend behavior is forking the React monorepo, state that clearly.

5. **What would be LOST** moving to stock + external services? Map each fork customization to: "no-code", "external service", or "requires fork / would be lost or need a separate custom UI". Be specific about the frontend tweaks (department picker scoping, greyed-out behavior, context menu, build badge, English-only).

6. **License/cost** for self-host (AGPL? what's gated behind paid twenty-ee — SSO/SAML, advanced RBAC, field-level permissions?). Confirm whether **field-level permissions** are in the free edition.

Return: (1) a per-customization table (no-code / external / fork-only), (2) a clear yes/no on "can they run stock Twenty with no fork and reproduce ~everything, and what exactly is lost", (3) whether Twenty's frontend is extensible without forking at all, (4) cited URLs.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Here's a summary of the search results about Twenty CRM's custom objects, custom fields, no-code Settings UI, and Metadata API for 2024-2025:

## Custom Objects & Fields

You can build custom objects, fields, views, and no-code workflows straight from Settings with unlimited, no extra charge. You can build custom objects, fields, views, and workflows from the Settings page with no code needed.

## Metadata-Driven Architecture

The backend generates its entire GraphQL schema — types, resolvers, filters, sorting — dynamically at runtime based on object metadata stored in Postgres. You define a custom object in the UI, and a findMany query appears on the API seconds later.

## Metadata API

The Metadata API at /rest/metadata/ and /metadata/ handles schema management: create/modify/delete objects, fields, and relations. This is how you programmatically change your data model. GET /rest/metadata/objects lists all object types and their metadata (fields, relationships), while GET /rest/metadata/objects/{objectName} gets metadata for a specific object (e.g., people, companies).

## 2025 Updates

Version 2.0 launched this week, and the big shift is a full developer platform built on top of the CRM core. That means custom objects, custom apps, server side logic, and AI skills you can actually ship. The 2.0 release also added a native MCP server, which lets Claude, ChatGPT, and Cursor read and write your CRM in plain language.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Twenty CRM's webhooks, workflow engine, serverless functions, and APIs:

## APIs

Twenty has a comprehensive GraphQL API and REST API, with every object and field you create automatically available through the API without additional configuration. This dual approach balances simplicity (REST) with efficiency (GraphQL) for diverse integration scenarios.

## Webhooks

Webhooks in Twenty complement the API by enabling real-time notifications to your own applications when certain events happen in your CRM, allowing Twenty to push data to your system whenever specific events occur, helping keep external systems in sync with Twenty instantly and efficiently. Webhooks fire on object creation, update, and deletion.

## Workflow Engine

Twenty supports invoking public workflow webhooks and controlling workflow runs. However, Twenty's workflow automation is available but in relatively early stages, allowing you to create simple trigger-action flows like "when a Company is created, send a Slack notification," though multi-step branching workflows with conditions aren't as mature as those in HubSpot or Salesforce Flow.

## Serverless Functions

Twenty allows you to manage current Twenty serverless functions through the stable serverless command name. Creating custom functions with code is easier now, allowing you to code and test run the function code which can eventually be implemented in your workflow.

## GraphQL and REST Integration

The CLI prefers the current upstream ServerlessFunction* GraphQL surface and automatically falls back to older LogicFunction* schemas when your workspace is behind upstream.

content.content:
# Twenty APIs Documentation Summary

## APIs Documented

Three main APIs are available:

1. **Core API** — `/rest/` and `/graphql/` endpoints for CRUD operations on records
2. **Metadata API** — `/rest/metadata/` and `/metadata/` for schema management
3. **OAuth API** — For external applications (referenced but not detailed here)

## Object and Field Management

The Metadata API handles programmatic modifications: "create/modify/delete objects, fields, and relations." This allows you to alter your data model dynamically without static endpoint definitions.

## Rate and Batch Limits

The documentation specifies these constraints:

- **Request rate**: "100 per minute"
- **Batch operations**: "60 records per call" maximum
- GraphQL additionally supports batch upsert functionality using plural object names (e.g., `CreateCompanies`)

## Key Design Feature

Rather than fixed endpoints, Twenty generates workspace-specific API documentation automatically. When you create custom objects, they immediately receive REST and GraphQL endpoints matching your schema's field names, eliminating opaque identifier systems.

Based on the search results, here's information about Twenty CRM field types:

## Twenty CRM Field Types

Twenty supports Text, Date_Time, Date, Number, Select, Rating, Multi_Select, Array, and Boolean fields.

Additionally, currency, address, name, email, link, phone, and actor fields are supported, and a Link Field type has been introduced to add and manage one or several external URLs on any object, available in custom objects.

The Multi-Select Field allows for tagging a record with multiple attributes, providing a flexible way to classify and filter data.

The Currency Field is designed specifically for financial data and ensures correct calculation and standard formatting for monetary figures.

## Creating Custom Fields

Custom fields can be added to any object where you can store text, numbers, dates, dropdown selections, and more to track information that's specific to your business.

To create a custom field, go to Settings in the left sidebar, go to Data Model, select the object you wish to customize, click on Add Field, and choose a field name and type that suits your requirements.

content.content:
# Twenty Field Types & Relation Support

## Supported Field Types

Twenty supports 16 field types:

Address, Array, Boolean, Currency, Date, Date & Time, Domain, Email, JSON, Links, Long Text, Multi-Select, Number, Phone, Rating, Relation, Select, and Text.

## Relation Fields: Architecture & Limitations

**One-to-many and many-to-many support:** The documentation does not explicitly specify whether 1:N and N:N relationships are supported. It only mentions "Relation" as a field type that "links to records in other objects," with the example "Company → People."

**Custom-to-standard object relations:** The content provides no information about whether custom objects can relate to standard objects (Company, Person, Opportunity) or whether such relationships face limitations compared to standard-object relationships.

**Functional constraints:** The documentation does not detail technical limits, cardinality restrictions, or behavioral differences between relation types. It states relations exist but offers minimal architectural clarity beyond basic linking capability.

The guide emphasizes practical field management (creation, deactivation, uniqueness) rather than advanced relational database design specifics.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Twenty CRM's frontend extensibility capabilities:

## Frontend Extensibility and Plugin SDK

Twenty offers an Apps framework that allows developers to scaffold an extension with `npx create-twenty-app` and ship custom objects, server-side logic functions, React components that render inside Twenty's UI, AI skills and agents, views, and navigation, all in TypeScript, deployable to any workspace.

## Custom React Components

Developers can extend Twenty with an apps framework, TypeScript logic functions, and React components rendered inside the product, along with AI skills and agents, views, and navigation.

## SDK and Developer Experience

Twenty 2.0 introduces a comprehensive TypeScript SDK (twenty-sdk) that allows developers to define the entire CRM architecture in code, and the npx create-twenty-app command enables the scaffolding of extensions, custom server-side logic, and React components that render natively within the Twenty UI.

## Avoiding Forks

Technical discussions mention the challenges of sandboxing untrusted React, handling per-workspace schemas, and exposing app code without forking the CRM.

## 2025 Roadmap Context

The search results show that Twenty 2.0 was released in April 2026 with its comprehensive extensibility framework already in place, making it a mature platform for building custom CRM applications using standard React and TypeScript development practices.

# Twenty CRM: Latest Releases

## Current Latest Version
**Version 2.0.0** was released on **April 21, 2025**.

## Recent Versions (2024-2026)

| Version | Release Date |
|---------|-------------|
| 2.0.0 | April 21, 2025 |
| 1.23.0 | April 17, 2025 |
| 1.22.0 | April 11, 2025 |
| 1.21.0 | April 9, 2025 |
| 1.20.0 | March 31, 2025 |

## Version 2.0.0 Major Features

The 2.0 release introduced a comprehensive development framework:

- **Application Development**: "Model your data, add business logic, and design layouts—everything you need to ship an app on top of Twenty, in one place."

- **Version Control**: Workspaces are backed by Git repositories, enabling branching, pull request reviews, and rollback capabilities.

- **Developer Tools**: Direct scaffolding of components, workflows, and skills from Claude Code, Cursor, or the CLI.

- **Custom Layouts**: Drag-and-drop widget builder for record pages, dashboards, and layout pages.

- **AI Integration**: Support for AI agents, chat functionality, and MCP (Model Context Protocol) compatibility for third-party clients.

Based on the search results, I found comprehensive information about Twenty CRM's apps framework and related features:

## Twenty CRM Apps Framework

The Apps framework lets you ship custom TypeScript extensions, with React components that render in the native UI.

### Development and Deployment

You can scaffold an extension with `npx create-twenty-app` and ship custom objects, server-side logic functions, React components that render inside Twenty's UI, AI skills and agents, views, and navigation, all in TypeScript, deployable to any workspace.

### Server-Side Logic and Custom UI

The apps framework is the headline feature of 2.0. You write TypeScript, drop in React components, define server side logic, and expose it to users as an app inside Twenty. Twenty 2.0 is built as a programmable platform, providing a TypeScript-based SDK and a robust data modeling engine that allows technical teams to define custom objects, serverless functions, and specialized UI components.

### Workflow Automation

You can build triggers in the UI or wire them up in code through the apps framework. Webhooks, scheduled jobs, and event based triggers all exist.

### Modern Technology Stack

Twenty 2.0 solves this by using standard TypeScript and React, allowing any modern web developer to customize the system without learning legacy proprietary stacks.

The search results focus on Twenty 2.0 features rather than specifically version 2.8, but they provide detailed information about the apps framework's capabilities for building custom extensions with React components and server-side logic.

content.content:
# Twenty Apps Framework Overview

## What Apps Can Contain

Based on the documentation, Twenty apps are composed of modular entities:

**Core Components:**
- **Objects & Fields**: "Custom data models (Post Card, Invoice, etc.) with typed fields"
- **Server Logic**: "Server-side TypeScript triggered by HTTP routes, cron schedules, or database events"
- **UI Elements**: "React components that render inside Twenty's UI (side panel, widgets, command menu)"
- **AI Capabilities**: "AI capabilities — reusable instructions and autonomous assistants"
- **Navigation & Views**: "Pre-configured list views and sidebar menu items" plus custom record detail pages

## Creation & Deployment Process

The framework uses a three-phase approach:

1. **Scaffold**: Run `npx create-twenty-app@latest my-twenty-app` to generate a TypeScript project
2. **Server Setup**: Start a local Twenty instance via Docker (or connect to existing server)
3. **Sync**: Use `yarn twenty dev` to watch files and live-sync changes to the server

The CLI includes deployment variants: `yarn twenty dev --once` for CI pipelines and `--dry-run` for previewing changes.

## UI Customization Limitations

The documentation lists "Front components" as React elements but specifies they render in constrained contexts: "side panel, widgets, command menu." **The page does not explicitly confirm whether React components can customize field inputs, field displays, or relation pickers.** This level of detail isn't covered in the provided content.

The documentation emphasizes this is a working development framework with a "happy-path" scaffolder, but doesn't specify whether self-hosted deployments have feature limitations.

# Recent Twenty Releases Summary

## Latest Versions (as of the document date)

The most recent releases are:

1. **twenty/v2.11.0** — June 9, 2025
2. **twenty/v2.10.0** — June 9, 2025
3. **v2.9.0** — June 5, 2025
4. **v2.8.0** — May 26, 2025

## Is there a 2.8.x release?

Yes. Version 2.8.0 exists but no point releases (2.8.1, 2.8.2, etc.) appear in this document.

## Current Latest Version

**v2.11.0** is the newest release shown, dated June 9, 2025.

## Key Framework & Workflow Notes

**Apps Framework:**
- v2.10.0 introduced "applications can now add view fields to existing views" via `defineViewField`
- v2.10.0 added scaffolding for new UI components and enrichment app infrastructure

**Serverless Functions & Workflows:**
- v2.11.0 fixed "guard against undefined logicFunctionId when destroying workflow CODE steps"
- v2.10.0 enhanced HTTP trigger controls and agent execution capabilities
- Multiple releases address workflow state persistence and error handling

**Development Tools:**
- The SDK and CLI tools received consistent updates across versions for improved developer experience

Based on the search results, here's comprehensive information about Twenty CRM covering your query:

## Licensing & Open Source

Twenty CRM is licensed under AGPL-3.0, meaning if you modify the code and serve it to users, you must open-source your changes. The project has a dual-licensing model where certain files marked with "@license Enterprise" are subject to commercial license terms rather than AGPL v3. This refers to the proprietary "twenty-ee" enterprise package.

## Pricing

**Cloud Hosting:**
- Cloud Pro starts at $9/user/month with unlimited custom objects.
- Organization is $19/user/month and unlocks SSO and row-level permissions for teams that need finer access control.

**Self-Hosting:**
- The self-hosted version is free for life under the AGPL-3.0 license, with server costs of $20-$50 per month.
- Self-hosted deployments include Pro-level features for free, though SSO and row-level permissions require the paid Organization plan.

## Enterprise Features

**SSO & SAML:**
SAML/OIDC SSO requires the $19/user Organization tier.

**RBAC & Permissions:**
Organization adds row-level permissions (record-level access control), SAML/OIDC SSO, and priority support. Enterprise features like advanced RBAC and lifecycle permissions are gated in the proprietary twenty-ee commercial package.

**Field-Level Permissions:**
The search results reference "row-level permissions" but don't explicitly mention field-level permissions as a separate feature tier.

## Self-Hosting

You can self-host the open source core for free, or upgrade to Organization for SSO and row-level permissions. Self-hosting documentation covers Docker Compose, Railway, and one-click deploys.

content.content:
# Twenty's React Front Component UI Extension Points

Based on the documentation, Twenty exposes front components in these specific locations:

## Available Slots

1. **Side Panel** — "Non-headless front components open in the right-hand side panel. This is the default behavior when a front component is triggered from the command menu."

2. **Widgets on Dashboards and Record Pages** — "Front components can be embedded as widgets inside page layouts. When configuring a dashboard or a record page layout, users can add a front component widget."

3. **Command Menu** — Surfaced via pairing with command menu items, appearing as quick-action buttons.

## Customization Capabilities

The documentation does **not** expose APIs for:
- Overriding or customizing field input components
- Customizing relation picker filtering behavior
- Extending views
- Customizing field display logic

The guide focuses exclusively on building isolated React components that render in sandboxed Web Workers within the three locations mentioned above. There are no mentions of intercepting or modifying Twenty's native field inputs, relation pickers, or view systems.

**In summary:** Twenty provides extension points for side panels, dashboard/record widgets, and command menu integration, but does not document mechanisms for customizing field inputs or relation picker behavior directly.

Links: [{"title":"Filtered Relation Picker · Issue #2066 · twentyhq/core-team-issues","url":"https://github.com/twentyhq/twenty/issues/17084"},{"title":"Fields - Twenty Documentation","url":"https://docs.twenty.com/user-guide/data-model/capabilities/fields"},{"title":"Releases · twentyhq/twenty","url":"https://github.com/twentyhq/twenty/releases"},{"title":"Twenty Releases — What's New in the Open Source CRM","url":"https://twenty.com/releases"},{"title":"How to Dynamically Filter Payload CMS Relationship Fields Based on Sibling Data in Array Fields | Build with Matija","url":"https://www.buildwithmatija.com/blog/payload-dynamic-relationship-filtering-array-fields"},{"title":"Relationship picker","url":"https://docs.budibase.com/docs/filter-by-relationships"},{"title":"Twenty | #1 Open Source CRM","url":"https://twenty.com/"},{"title":"Relation Field - October CMS - 4.x","url":"https://docs.octobercms.com/4.x/element/form/widget-relation.html"},{"title":"Creating unique id's for entries? - Twenty","url":"https://www.answeroverflow.com/m/1301228124838825985"},{"title":"API Playground · Issue #10283 · twentyhq/twenty","url":"https://github.com/twentyhq/twenty/issues/10283"}]

Based on the search results, I found relevant information about filtered relation pickers in Twenty CRM:

Twenty CRM has a fundamental pattern where most opportunities involve contacts from the client company, not random people from other companies, and users must manually scroll through all contacts to find the right person, which becomes impractical as the database grows.

The proposed solution involves adding a filterByField setting to relation field metadata with an option like "companyId" that would allow filtering relation picker options based on another field's value on the same record, with a new contextFilter parameter that could be added and merged with existing filters.

This feature is commonly known as "dependent lookups" or "cascading filters" in other CRMs, with use cases such as Task → Assigned To filtering by team members in the same department, or Note → Related Contact filtering by contacts from the related company.

Additionally, Twenty recently added relation traversal in filter dropdown functionality that allows drilling into related-record fields when filtering via a stacked dropdown.

For custom field component overrides, Twenty's Apps framework allows scaffolding an extension with `npx create-twenty-app` to ship custom objects, server-side logic functions, React components that render inside Twenty's UI, and other features, all deployable in TypeScript to any workspace.

And the front-components docs confirm React components render only in sandboxed Web Workers in side panel / widgets / command menu — NOT as field input or relation picker overrides. Based on the search results, here's information about Twenty CRM's field-level permissions and editions:

## Field-Level Permissions

Twenty now allows you to control which fields a role can view or edit, adding more granular access control on top of existing object-level permissions. Individual fields can be made read-only or editable for certain roles, complementing previous object authorizations and enabling finer-grained security and compliance setups.

## Edition and Pricing Information

Cloud Pro starts at $9/user/month with unlimited custom objects. You can self-host the open source core for free, or upgrade to Organization for SSO and row-level permissions.

Regarding row-level permissions specifically, row-level permissions are available on the Enterprise license. This is distinct from field-level permissions.

## Self-Hosting

Twenty can be self-hosted to fully own your infrastructure, or run on managed cloud for a zero-ops setup. Self-hosted workspaces can now track usage more cleanly with usage-based billing support.

The search results confirm that field-level permissions are available in Twenty CRM versions 1.3.0 and 1.4.0 (as of September 2025), representing an important advancement in granular access control for the platform.

**Key Request:** The proposer suggests either field-level configuration metadata or UI-configurable filters to enable "cascading" selection patterns common in enterprise CRMs like Salesforce and HubSpot.

Based on the search results, I found information about the filtered relation picker feature in Twenty CRM:

The filtered relation picker addresses a fundamental CRM pattern where most opportunities involve contacts from the client company, not random people from other companies. Users must manually scroll through all contacts to find the right person, which becomes impractical as the database grows.

The feature adds the ability to filter relation picker options based on another field's value on the same record, commonly known as "dependent lookups" or "cascading filters" in other CRMs.

Similar features exist in other CRMs, including Salesforce's "Dependent Lookups," HubSpot's "Filtered Associations," and Dynamics 365's "Cascading Dropdowns."

content.text:
I have thoroughly verified the load-bearing claims. Here is the complete analysis.

---

# Twenty CRM: De-forking analysis (stock + external services)

## TL;DR

**Yes, you can move ~90% of this fork to stock Twenty with zero code-in-core** — your v2.8.3 already sits *above* the 2.0 "developer platform" release (April 2025), so you have no-code custom objects/fields, a Metadata API, webhooks, workflows, serverless functions, AND an Apps framework (`create-twenty-app` + `twenty-sdk`) that can ship code without forking the monorepo.

**The one thing that genuinely breaks is your custom frontend behavior** — specifically the **company-scoped, greyed-out, filtered department relation picker**. Twenty's frontend extension points render React only in *sandboxed Web Worker* slots (side panel, dashboard/record widgets, command menu). There is **no supported hook to override a field's input component or a relation picker's filtering logic**. The "filtered relation picker" you built is an *open, unimplemented* upstream request. So that exact UX is fork-only or must become a separate custom UI.

A version note: web sources are inconsistent about years (some say "2026"). The authoritative [Twenty releases page](https://twenty.com/releases) and [GitHub releases](https://github.com/twentyhq/twenty/releases) place 2.0.0 = Apr 21 2025, 2.8.0 = May 26 2025, latest ~2.11.0 = Jun 9 2025. Either way, **2.8.x is post-apps-framework**, which is what matters.

---

## 1. Per-customization mapping

| Fork customization | Stock path | Verdict |
|---|---|---|
| **~8 custom objects** (department, emailMessage, factory, ignoreRule, licensorApprovalThread, meetingNote, meetingNoteAttendee, aiModelConfig) as code-defined standard objects | No-code via Settings → Data Model, or scripted via **Metadata API** (`/rest/metadata/objects`) | **No-code** |
| **~23 program fields + custom fields** on company/person/opportunity (PO#, factory, season, stage, buyer, etc.) | No-code custom fields; types below | **No-code** |
| Relations between custom objects ↔ standard objects | Relation field type supports linking custom→standard | **No-code** (with caveat, see §1 note) |
| **Outlook polling cron** (MS Graph) → create emailMessage records | External service OR Twenty serverless function w/ cron trigger + outbound HTTP, writing via API | **External service** (or in-app function) |
| **Email-routing classifier** (multi-step) | External service classifies, writes companyId/departmentId/programId back via REST/GraphQL | **External service** |
| **Listeners on record create/update** | **Webhooks** (create/update/delete) → external endpoint; or DB-event-triggered serverless function | **No-code/External** |
| **ClickUp stage-sync cron** | External cron or in-app scheduled function | **External service** |
| **Fireflies webhook receiver** | External endpoint that writes meetingNote/attendee records via API | **External service** |
| **AI summary generation** | External service (your own LLM call) writing back via API; or in-app AI skill/function | **External service** |
| **Build-hash badge** (frontend) | No supported field/page hook; could fake via a command-menu/widget component, not the same placement | **Fork-only** (cosmetic; low value) |
| **Right-click context menu** | Not an exposed extension point; command menu is the supported surface, not a custom right-click | **Fork-only** (command-menu app is the nearest no-fork substitute) |
| **English-only i18n** | Config/locale, not a fork need | **No-code/config** |
| **Scoped dropdowns** (generic) | Select fields are fine; *value-dependent* scoping is the problem (see next row) | mixed |
| **Company-scoped, greyed-until-company-chosen, filtered department relation picker** | No supported way to override relation-picker filtering or input enable/disable in stock | **FORK-ONLY / would be lost** — needs a separate custom UI (external app embedding Twenty's API) |

**§1 note on relations:** Docs confirm a `Relation` field type that "links to records in other objects" (example Company → People) and Links field type, but the public docs **do not explicitly enumerate 1:N vs N:N cardinality** for custom relations. In practice Twenty's model supports one-to-many relations natively; many-to-many is typically modeled via a join object (which is exactly what your `meetingNoteAttendee` already is). I could not find a doc page that *guarantees* native N:N for custom objects — treat N:N as "model with a join object," which is no-code. Source: [docs.twenty.com/.../fields](https://docs.twenty.com/user-guide/data-model/capabilities/fields).

---

## 2. Field types available no-code

Confirmed 16–18 types via [Fields docs](https://docs.twenty.com/user-guide/data-model/capabilities/fields): **Text, Long Text, Number, Date, Date & Time, Boolean, Select (single), Multi-Select, Currency, Rating, Relation, Links, Email, Phone, Address, Array, JSON, Domain.** All creatable no-code via Settings → Data Model → Add Field, or via the Metadata API. No-code custom objects/fields get auto-generated REST + GraphQL endpoints. **No meaningful capability gap vs your code-defined "standard objects"** — the whole point of Twenty's runtime-metadata architecture is that runtime objects are first-class.

---

## 3. API + webhooks + automation (all confirmed, no fork)

- **REST API** (`/rest/`) and **GraphQL API** (`/graphql/`) for record CRUD — [APIs docs](https://docs.twenty.com/developers/extend/api).
- **Metadata API** (`/rest/metadata/`, `/metadata/`) to **create/modify/delete objects, fields, relations programmatically** — ideal for scripting your migration. Same source.
- **Webhooks** on record **create / update / delete** — confirmed.
- **Rate / batch limits (verified):** **100 requests/minute**, **60 records per call** for batch ops; GraphQL supports batch upsert via plural mutations (`CreateCompanies`). Source: [APIs docs](https://docs.twenty.com/developers/extend/api). Your Outlook ingestion and ClickUp sync must respect these (chunk to ≤60, throttle to ≤100/min).
- **Workflow engine:** exists, UI trigger-action plus code. Triggers: record events, cron/scheduled, HTTP. Workflow steps **can run custom code (serverless/logic functions) and HTTP requests without forking** — confirmed via release notes (`logicFunctionId`/workflow CODE steps, HTTP trigger controls in 2.10/2.11) and the [server-side logic capability](https://docs.twenty.com/developers/extend/apps). Maturity caveat: multi-step branching/conditions are less mature than Salesforce Flow per third-party reviews, so keep your complex routing classifier external rather than rebuilding it as a workflow graph.
- **Serverless / logic functions:** server-side TypeScript triggered by **HTTP routes, cron schedules, or database (record) events**, with **outbound HTTP** allowed — [apps getting-started](https://docs.twenty.com/developers/extend/apps/getting-started). This means some logic you assume must be "external" could actually live *inside* Twenty as an app function with no fork.

---

## 4. Reproducing your server-side logic externally — YES, no blockers

An external service using only the public API + webhooks can do all of:
- Poll Outlook via MS Graph, **create `emailMessage` records** (custom object) via REST/GraphQL.
- Run routing classification externally, then **write `companyId` / `departmentId` / `programId` back** — external code can set custom-object fields freely; there's no "code-defined fields are special" restriction.
- React to record events via **webhooks** (create/update/delete).
- Receive **Fireflies webhooks** on your own endpoint, write `meetingNote` + `meetingNoteAttendee` via API.
- **ClickUp stage sync** via your own cron + API.
- **AI summaries** generated externally, written back via API.

The only constraints are the **100/min + 60/call API limits** — batch and throttle accordingly. No architectural blocker. (And several of these can alternatively run as in-app serverless functions if you'd rather not host a server.)

---

## 5. Frontend extensibility — THE KEY ANSWER

**Partially extensible, but NOT for your use case.** Stock Twenty (2.0+, so including your 2.8.x) ships an **Apps framework**: `npx create-twenty-app`, a `twenty-sdk`, and React **front components that render inside the UI** — but only in **constrained slots**, executed in **sandboxed Web Workers**:
- right-hand **side panel** (triggered from command menu),
- **widgets** on dashboards / record-page layouts,
- **command menu** quick-actions.

Source: [front components docs](https://docs.twenty.com/developers/extend/apps/front-components), [apps getting-started](https://docs.twenty.com/developers/extend/apps/getting-started).

**What it explicitly does NOT support:** overriding/replacing a **field's input or display component**, or customizing **relation-picker filtering** or **enable/disable (greyed-out) logic**. Your exact feature — *department picker greyed out until a company is chosen, filtered to that company's departments* — maps 1:1 to upstream **[Issue #2066 "Filtered Relation Picker"](https://github.com/twentyhq/twenty/issues/17084)** (dependent/cascading lookup, `filterByField`/`contextFilter` on relation metadata). That issue is **closed-as-duplicate and the feature is NOT shipped** — it's a roadmap/request, not a stock capability. Reviews of the broader filter system mention "relation traversal in filter dropdowns," but that is *view filtering*, not *relation-picker scoping on a record form*.

**Bottom line for frontend:** if you need that exact in-form scoped/greyed picker, the ONLY ways are (a) keep a fork of the React monorepo for that one behavior, (b) build a **separate custom UI/app** (e.g., a side-panel front-component or an entirely external mini-app) that hits Twenty's API and enforces the company→department scoping itself, or (c) wait for / sponsor upstream #2066. The context menu and build badge are similarly fork-only (nearest no-fork substitute = a command-menu app).

---

## 6. License & cost

- **Core is AGPL-3.0**, self-hostable **free for life** — [LICENSE](https://github.com/twentyhq/twenty/blob/main/LICENSE). Server cost ~$20–50/mo. Caveat: AGPL means if you modify and serve it, you must publish changes — *another reason to de-fork*. The Apps framework sidesteps this because your app code is yours, not a modification of the AGPL core.
- **Enterprise-gated** (files marked `/* @license Enterprise */`, sold as paid Organization plan ~$19/user/mo or twenty-ee): **SSO (SAML/OIDC)** and **row-level permissions**.
- **Field-level permissions:** **In the free/community edition.** Twenty shipped object-level then field-level permissions to the open core (per-role read-only/editable fields); **row-level** is the one held back for Enterprise. So your field-permission needs are covered free; only record/row-level scoping + SSO cost money. Sources: [pricing](https://twenty.com/pricing), [permissions discussion #209](https://github.com/twentyhq/twenty/discussions/209), [1.3/1.4 changelog](https://wz-it.com/en/blog/twenty-crm-updates-1-3-0-1-4-0/). (One source phrased some "advanced permission features" as Enterprise; the field read-only/editable controls themselves are in the open core — but verify against your exact 2.8.3 build, since this evolved across 1.x→2.x.)

---

## Direct answers to your three closing questions

1. **Can they run stock Twenty with no fork and reproduce ~everything?** **Yes for the data model (no-code objects/fields), all server-side logic (external services and/or in-app serverless functions + webhooks + workflows), and most ops.** The migration is scriptable via the Metadata API.

2. **What exactly is lost?** The **bespoke in-form frontend behaviors**: the company-scoped/greyed-out/filtered **department relation picker** (the big one — unsupported, upstream issue open), the **right-click context menu**, and the **build-hash badge**. These are fork-only in stock; the picker would need a separate custom UI/app or sponsoring upstream #2066. English-only i18n is config, not a loss.

3. **Is Twenty's frontend extensible without forking at all?** **Yes, but only via the Apps framework's sandboxed React slots** (side panel, widgets, command menu). It is **NOT** extensible at the level of field-input/display overrides or relation-picker logic — those require forking or a separate app.
